title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
Overall Goal

To outline a methodology for designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
Outline
Part I: Theories of Memory Processing and Learning

Information Processing Theory
Cognitive Load Theory

Part II: Cognitive Load Theory and Course Design

Identify Learner Personas / Types
Mental Models
Formative Assessments
Improving Course Content and Delivery

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the main types of cognitive load and the limits of human memory.
Explain the differences between various learner types / personas.
Define mental models and create concept maps.
Create formative assessments that diagnose flawed mental models.

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text: Example: Prof. X’s Biochemistry 101 Lecture (A)

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
Example: Prof. X’s Biochemistry 101 Lecture (B)


https://monicanasseri.wixsite.com/biochemical-pathways/biochemical-pathways

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
1. Information Processing Theory

A framework for understanding how information is encoded into memory




Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968) Psychology of Learning and Motivation (2) 89-195
https://www.simplypsychology.org/multi-store.html

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
2. Cognitive Load

Available amount of information that the working / short-term memory can hold at a specific time (7 +/- 2 chunks of info at a time)




Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968) Psychology of Learning and Motivation (2) 89-195
https://www.simplypsychology.org/multi-store.html

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
3. Cognitive Load Theory

Describes how the human mind processes new information
3 Main Types of Cognitive Load






Sweller, J. (1998) Cognitive Science 12(2): 257-285
https://mcdreeamiemusings.com/blog/2019/10/15/the-good-the-bad-and-the-can-be-ugly-the-three-parts-of-cognitive-load

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
3. Cognitive Load Theory
Intrinsic

Innate difficulty of a task and complexity of new information.
Influenced by age and/or educational background.




Sweller, J. (1998) Cognitive Science 12(2): 257-285
https://mcdreeamiemusings.com/blog/2019/10/15/the-good-the-bad-and-the-can-be-ugly-the-three-parts-of-cognitive-load

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
3. Cognitive Load Theory
Extraneous

Load due to poorly designed instructional material.
Distracts working memory from processing the required information.



Sweller, J. (1998) Cognitive Science 12(2): 257-285
https://mcdreeamiemusings.com/blog/2019/10/15/the-good-the-bad-and-the-can-be-ugly-the-three-parts-of-cognitive-load

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
3. Cognitive Load Theory
Extraneous

Examples




Sweller, J. (1998) Cognitive Science 12(2): 257-285
https://mcdreeamiemusings.com/blog/2019/10/15/the-good-the-bad-and-the-can-be-ugly-the-three-parts-of-cognitive-load

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
3. Cognitive Load Theory
Germane

Mental capacity used to integrate new information with existing knowledge
Effort required to learn and retain the material
Influenced by level of training (Math Professor vs 1st grader)



Sweller, J. (1998) Cognitive Science 12(2): 257-285
https://mcdreeamiemusings.com/blog/2019/10/15/the-good-the-bad-and-the-can-be-ugly-the-three-parts-of-cognitive-load

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
1. Identify Learner Personas / Types

Learners can be distinguished by the mental models they use to solve problem.




https://carpentries.github.io/instructor-training/
Benner P. (2004) Bulletin of Science, Technology & Society, 24(3), 188–199

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
2. Mental models

“A simplified representation of the most important parts of some problem domain that is good enough to enable problem solving.”



Limitations

Simplified representation.
Atoms are not balls
Sticks are not bonds.




https://commoncog.com/teaching-tech-together/
https://education.riaus.org.au/a-better-way-to-see-molecules/

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
2. Mental models
  

https://carpentries.github.io/instructor-training/

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text: 2. Mental models

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
3. Formative Assessments

https://carpentries.github.io/instructor-training/

Help to identify common misconceptions and broken mental models.

Factual errors: The Capital City of Sweden is Doha.
Broken models: Motion and acceleration must always be in the same direction.
Fundamental beliefs: Some people are computational and others are not.

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
3. Formative Assessments
Example

Each incorrect answer has diagnostic power and will guide correction

text:

text:
4. Improving course content and delivery
Reduce split-attention effect



MindTools: https://www.mindtools.com/aqxwcpa/cognitive-load-theory

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
4. Improving course content and delivery
Encourage the use of concept maps
Example Discussion: Renewable and Non-renewable Energy Sources


https://visme.co/blog/how-to-make-a-concept-map/

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
4. Improving course content and delivery

Go slow and repeat if necessary
Be aware of expert blind spots
Use authentic tasks and examples to teach
Give and receive appropriate feedback


https://carpentries.github.io/instructor-training/

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
Conclusions

Cognitive load and working / short-term memory have a significant impact on learning outcomes.
Knowledge of learner types and personas and the mental models they use is critical for effective teaching.
Formative assessments should be incorporated into lessons to identify misconceptions and receive teaching feedback.

title: Designing courses that minimize cognitive overload in learners
text:
Discussion Questions

Where have you identified “expert blind spots” in your own teaching?
How can you reduce cognitive overload in the subjects that you teach?
Based on what you have learnt, how will you structure your courses differently?

title: Sample Biomaterials Syllabus
text:
Prerequisites: TBD
Lectures: TBD
Tutorials: TBD
Laboratory Sessions: TBD
Instructor: William Okech, PhD
Email Me
Course Website: https://wokech.github.io –> Courses –> Select Course


Biomaterials play an important role in the life sciences and medicine. They are used to aid in the restoration of function and promote healing after injury and disease. Biomaterials can be of natural, synthetic, or hybrid origin, and they are designed to interact with living systems in a safe and compatible manner. In this course, we will look at various materials including metals, ceramics, plastics, and composites, as well as living cells and tissues that are used to develop biomaterials. Additionally, the course will explore material characterization and testing methods, biocompatibility, and important considerations for the design and manufacture of materials for biomedical applications. In addition to lectures (where relevant), laboratory activities and demonstrations will be performed. Lastly, case studies and contemporary examples will be used to explore new technologies.




History and Current Uses of Biomaterials
Atomic Theory of Materials
Mechanics of Materials
Surface vs Bulk Properties of Materials
Biomaterial-Host Tissue Interactions
Material Degradation
Major Biomaterial Groups (Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, and Composites)
Phase Diagrams and Phase Transformations
Drug Delivery Systems and Tissue Engineering





Lectures: TBD
Tutorials: TBD
Laboratory Exercises: TBD





Regular Examination at end of Semester: TBD
Continuous Assessment: TBD
The students must complete and pass all the labs before they can be allowed to proceed to the next class.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Overall Goal
To outline a methodology for developing a computational research skill instruction program centered in libraries that will support learners with diverse disciplinary and educational backgrounds.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Outline

Importance of computational research skills
Effective teaching of computational research skills
Sample lesson and problem
Challenges associated with teaching computational research skills
Conclusions

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Importance of Computational Research Skills
Why are computational research skills important for everyone?

“Computational thinking is a fundamental skill for everyone, not just computer scientists.” (Wing, 2006)


Wing, J (2006) Comm. ACM, 49 (3):33-35

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Importance of Computational Research Skills
Why are computational research skills important for everyone?

Major components of computational thinking include:

Decomposition – break down a complex problem to multiple simpler problems.
Pattern recognition – identify patterns in the data or information.



Grover, S and Pea, R (2012) Educational Researcher, 42 (1):38-43

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Importance of Computational Research Skills
Why are computational research skills important for everyone?

Major components of computational thinking include:

Abstraction – identify and use the relevant aspects of the problem.
Algorithmic thinking – design algorithms to solve problems.



Grover, S and Pea, R (2012) Educational Researcher, 42 (1):38-43

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Importance of Computational Research Skills
Why are computational research skills important for everyone?

New career opportunities and increased competitiveness in the job market.

Employment of software developers is projected to grow by 25% (~400k jobs) between 2021 and 2031 (Bureau of Labor Statistics).



https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm#tab-6

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Importance of Computational Research Skills
Why are computational research skills important for everyone?

Subject-matter experts with computational research skills are required to build new tools/technologies. Examples include:

Computational linguists – develop new language-learning apps.
Data journalists – develop fact-checking and misinformation detectors.



https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm#tab-6

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Importance of Computational Research Skills
What computational research skills are important?

This can be determined with a needs analysis using:

Surveys and Questionnaires sent to humanities departments,
Literature reviews focused on computing education research,
Interviews with faculty and staff from humanities departments.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Summary

Computational research skills are essential for everyone.
Humanities students can greatly benefit from computational research skills by:

Exploring new research directions, and,
Increasing their employment options.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
Recognize the diversity of needs

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
What activities should the instruction program focus on?

Workshops - where the most common computational tasks and the needs identified by graduate students are taught.
Office hours - supervised by an undergraduate/graduate student with a background in computational research.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
What activities should the instruction program focus on?

Mentoring - pair humanities graduate students with computational science students.
Bootcamps - focused on specific data analysis tools/programs.
Journal/Data clubs - where new and relevant tools, techniques, and software packages will be demonstrated.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills

A number of independent initiatives exist to train the general public in basic data and computational skills. These include:

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
What should novice coders learn? (My personal opinion)

Scratch (block-based visual programming language) for complete novices.
Help new coders learn the basics of programming, such as loops, conditionals, and variables.



Scratch: https://scratch.mit.edu/

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
What should novice coders learn? (My personal opinion)

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
Key points for teaching novice coders

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
Key points for teaching novice coders

Use live coding.
Embrace coding errors as an opportunity to teach debugging.
Use “worked examples” and pace learning to avoid cognitive overload.
Encourage working in pairs and group coding.
Promote daily practice.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Effective teaching of computational research skills
text:
Effective teaching of computational research skills
Key points for teaching novice coders

Learn student personas and adapt lessons to suit each student.
Give effective feedback that motivates.
Create learning goals for students.
Use real-life tasks relevant to student research.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Challenges associated with teaching computational research skills
text:
Challenges associated with teaching computational research skills
Barriers to overcome include:

Attitudes towards coding.
The variety of needs (even within humanities):

Example: Psychology vs Literature.

Over-reliance on no-code commercially available tools and a lack of willingness to change.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
section: Challenges associated with teaching computational research skills
text:
Challenges associated with teaching computational research skills
What students should avoid:

Learning too much at once without mastering the basics.
Continuously working on tutorials but not applying knowledge to personal projects.
Copy-and-pasting code rather than understanding why the code was used.
Learning multiple tools/programs simultaneously (such as Python, R, and Javascript).

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Conclusions

Computational thinking is for everyone.
Computational research skills can be very helpful for humanities students.
Various initiatives such as the Carpentries and CodeRefinery have devised effective blueprints for teaching.

title: Developing library-based computational research skill instruction programs
text:
Conclusions

Novices should consider starting with visual block-based programming.
There is a need to teach students effective learning practices and overcome the barriers to coding.
Teaching should be adapted to different student abilities and should include motivational feedback.

title: Hello!
text:
Hi there! Welcome to my teaching, training, and research website.

Teaching
This is a repository for all my current teaching materials.


Training
In addition to university teaching, I am also a Certified Carpentries Instructor and a trainer with the Digital Research Academy. As a member of these organizations, my goal is to train learners on the basics of computational thinking and promote the use of open-source tools (such as R/Python) to solve data analysis and visualization problems.
Resources


Research Interests

Vascular Biology
Sports Performance Measurement and Analytics
Biomedical Data Science Education for Low-Resource Settings

Description of Research Interests

title: Readings for the Young Scientist
text: This page contains a list of readings that I would recommend for upper-level undergraduate and graduate students getting started in STEM research. It includes articles focused on best practices, important research papers, and science integrity.

title: Readings for the Young Scientist
text:
Best Practices

How to succeed in science: a concise guide for young biomedical scientists. Part I: taking the plunge. Yewdell JW, Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol. (2008).
How to succeed in science: a concise guide for young biomedical scientists. Part II: making discoveries. Yewdell JW, Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol. (2008).
The importance of stupidity in scientific research. Schwartz MA, J Cell Sci. (2008).
Unskilled and unaware of it: how difficulties in recognizing one’s own incompetence lead to inflated self-assessments (Dunning-Kruger Effect). Kruger J and Dunning D, J Pers Soc Psychol. (1999).
How to choose a good scientific problem. Alon U, Mol Cell. (2009).
PLOS “Ten Simple Rules” Collection provides a quick, concentrated guide for mastering some of the professional challenges research scientists face in their careers. PloS.
Resources for PhD Students Guillaume Dalle.
Pessimistic scientific articles by Abhishaike Mahajan
The Codon Guide to Synthetic Biology
How to Read a Scientific Paper
How To Read Scientific And Research Papers: Advice For Journalists

title: Readings for the Young Scientist
text:
Challenges

The Emotional Toll of Graduate School. Puri P, Sci Am. (2019).
PhDs: the tortuous truth. Woolston C, Nature (2019).
Why doing a PhD is often a waste of time The Economist, Medium (2016).
There’s an awful cost to getting a PhD that no one talks about Walker J, Quartz (2015).
Strategies for Supporting Engineering Student Mental Health Wilson & Goldberg, CEE (2023).
Prioritizing mental health and wellness in engineering S. Wilson, ASEE (2022)
Falling behind: postdocs in their thirties tire of putting life on hold

title: Readings for the Young Scientist
text:
Statistics

What exactly is ‘N’ in cell culture and animal experiments? Lazic SE et al., PloS Biol. (2018).
SuperPlots: Communicating reproducibility and variability in cell biology. Lord SJ et al., J Cell Biol. (2020).
Ten common statistical mistakes to watch out for when writing or reviewing a manuscript. Makin TR et al. eLife (2019).
Beyond bar and line graphs: time for a new data presentation paradigm. Weissgerber TL et al. PloS Biol. (2015).
Statistical relevance—relevant statistics, part I. Klaus B EMBO J. (2015).
Statistical relevance-relevant statistics, part II: presenting experimental data. Klaus B EMBO J. (2015).
Ten simple rules for getting started with statistics in graduate school Zitomer RA et al. PloS Comput Biol. (2022).

title: Readings for the Young Scientist
text:
Writing

ACS Guide to Scholarly Communication. ACS. (2020).
The art of writing science. Plaxco KW, Prot. Sci. (2010).
Whitesides’ Group: Writing a Paper. Whitesides GM, Adv. Mat. (2004).
How to write a first-class paper. Gewin V, Nature (2018).
Writing readable prose: When planning a scientific manuscript, following a few simple rules has a large impact. Bredan AS & van Roy F, EMBO Rep. (2006).
Ten simple rules for structuring papers Mensh B & Kording K, PLoS Comp Biol. (2017).
Write accessibly Editors, Nat Biomed Eng. (2021).
Ten simple rules for scientists engaging in science communication
Science communication in experimental biology: experiences and recommendations

title: Readings for the Young Scientist
text:
Science Integrity

Science Integrity Digest Bik E
Brookes Lab Brookes P. Sample Post focused on “Fake Data”
PubPeer: The Online Journal Club
For Better Science Schneider L
Retraction Watch
Automatic detection of image manipulations in the biomedical literature Bucci EM, Cell Death and Disease (2018).
Learning to identify image manipulations in scientific publications Mazaheri G et al., arXiv (2021).
Publishing ethics in the era of paper mills Hackett R and Kelly S, Biol Open (2020).

text:

Lancaster et al. (2014). Generation of cerebral organoids from human pluripotent stem cells. Nature Protocols.

text:

Li et al. (2016). Designing hydrogels for controlled drug delivery. Nature Reviews Materials.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
text:
Biomechanics

Hill (1938). The heat of shortening and the dynamic constants of muscle. Proc Biol Sci.
Fung (1967). Elasticity of soft tissues in simple elongation. J Physiology.
Mow et al. (1980). Biphasic Creep and Stress Relaxation of Articular Cartilage in Compression: Theory and Experiments. J Biomech Eng.
Dewey Jr. et al (1981) The Dynamic Response of Vascular Endothelial Cells to Fluid Shear Stress. J Biomech Eng.
Guharay and Sachs (1984). Stretch-activated single ion channel currents in tissue-cultured embryonic chick skeletal muscle.. J Physiology.
Ku et al (1985). Pulsatile flow and atherosclerosis in the human carotid bifurcation. Positive correlation between plaque location and low oscillating shear stress. Arteriosclerosis, Thrombosis, and Vascular Biology.
Wang et al. (1993). Mechanotransduction Across the Cell Surface and Through the Cytoskeleton. Science.
Ingber (1993). Cellular tensegrity: defining new rules of biological design that govern the cytoskeleton. J Cell Science.
Dembo and Wang (1999). Stresses at the Cell-to-Substrate Interface during Locomotion of Fibroblasts. Biophysics Journal.
Paszek et al. (2005). Tensional homeostasis and the malignant phenotype. Cancer Cell.
Taylor et al. (2009). Patient-Specific Modeling of Cardiovascular Mechanics. Ann Rev Biomed Eng.
Dupont et al. (2011). Role of YAP/TAZ in mechanotransduction. Nature.
Humphrey et al. (2021). Vascular Mechanobiology: Homeostasis, Adaptation, and Disease. Ann Rev Biomed Eng.

text:

House et al. (1993). Long Term Results of Electrode Implantation and Electronic Stimulation of the Cochlea in Man. Annals of Otology, Rhinology & Laryngology.
Nicolelis et al. (2001). Actions from thoughts. Nature.
Ferris et al. (2005). Powered Lower Limb Orthoses for Gait Rehabilitation. Topics in Spinal Cord Rehabilitation.
Hochberg et al. (2006). Neuronal ensemble control of prosthetic devices by a human with tetraplegia. Nature.
Velliste et al. (2008). Cortical control of a prosthetic arm for self-feeding. Nature.
Kuiken et al. (2009). Targeted muscle reinnervation for real-time myoelectric control of multifunction artificial arms. JAMA.
Musk et al. (2019). An Integrated Brain-Machine Interface Platform With Thousands of Channels. J Med Internet Res.
Park et al. (2024). In-vivo integration of soft neural probes through high-resolution printing of liquid electronics on the cranium. Nature Communications.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
section: Biomedical Instrumentation, Signal Processing, and Medical Devices
text:
Biomedical Instrumentation, Signal Processing, and Medical Devices

Chardack et al. (1960). A transistorized, self-contained, implantable pacemaker for the long-term correction of complete heart block. Surgery.
Sutton et al. (1965). Evoked-Potential Correlates of Stimulus Uncertainty. Science.
Bruner (1969). Hazards of Electrical Apparatus. Anesthesiology.
Akselrod et al. (1981). Power Spectrum Analysis of Heart Rate Fluctuation: A Quantitative Probe of Beat-to-Beat Cardiovascular Control. Science.
Winter and Webster (1983). Reduction of Interference Due to Common Mode Voltage in Biopotential Amplifiers. IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering.
Pan et al. (1985). A Real-Time QRS Detection Algorithm. IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering.
Mallat (1989). A theory for multiresolution signal decomposition: the wavelet representation. IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence.
Krishnan et al. (2022). Continuous, noninvasive wireless monitoring of flow of cerebrospinal fluid through shunts in patients with hydrocephalus. npj Digital Medicine.
Chen et al. (2022). A wireless millimetric magnetoelectric implant for the endovascular stimulation of peripheral nerves. Nature Biomedical Engineering.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
text:
Biosensors

Updike and Hicks. (1967). The Enzyme Electrode. Nature.
Ellington and Szostak (1990). In vitro selection of RNA molecules that bind specific ligands. Nature.
Thévenot et al. (2001). Electrochemical biosensors: recommended definitions and classification. **.
Gootenberg et al. (2017). Nucleic acid detection with CRISPR-Cas13a/C2c2. Science.
Chen et al. (2018). CRISPR-Cas12a target binding unleashes indiscriminate single-stranded DNase activity. Science.
Dervisevic et al. (2025). High-density microneedle array-based wearable electrochemical biosensor for detection of insulin in interstitial fluid. Biosensors and Bioelectronics.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
text:
Biomedical Imaging and Optics

Ogawa et al. (1990). Brain magnetic resonance imaging with contrast dependent on blood oxygenation. PNAS.
Puppels et al. (1990). Studying single living cells and chromosomes by confocal Raman microspectroscopy. Nature.
Huang et al. (1991). Optical Coherence Tomography. Science.
Weissleder and Ntziachristos (2003). Shedding light onto live molecular targets. Nature Medicine.
Contag and Bachmann (2002). Advances in In Vivo Bioluminescence Imaging of Gene Expression. Annual Review of Biomedical Engineering.
Litjens et al. (2017). A survey on deep learning in medical image analysis. Medical Image Analysis.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
text:
Microscopy and Image Analysis

Marton (1934). Electron Microscopy of Biological Objects. Nature.
Coons et al. (1942). The Demonstration of Pneumococcal Antigen in Tissues by the Use of Fluorescent Antibody. The Journal of Immunology.
Oatley et al. (1966). Scanning Electron Microscopy. Advances in Electronics and Electron Physics.
Davidovits and Egger (1971). Scanning Laser Microscope for Biological Investigations. Appl. Opt..
Axelrod et al. (1976). Mobility measurement by analysis of fluorescence photobleaching recovery kinetics. Biophysics Journal.
Denk et al. (1990). Two-Photon Laser Scanning Fluorescence Microscopy. Science.
Chalfie et al. (1994). Green Fluorescent Protein as a Marker for Gene Expression. Science.
Hell and Wichmann (1994). Breaking the diffraction resolution limit by stimulated emission: stimulated-emission-depletion fluorescence microscopy. Opt Lett.
Huisken et al. (2004). Optical Sectioning Deep Inside Live Embryos by Selective Plane Illumination Microscopy. Science.
Chen et al. (2015). Expansion microscopy. Science.
Weigert et al. (2018). Content-aware image restoration: pushing the limits of fluorescence microscopy. Nature Methods.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
text:
Physiology for Engineers

Hodgkin and Huxley (1952). A quantitative description of membrane current and its application to conduction and excitation in nerve. J Physiol.
Womersley et al. (1955). Method for the calculation of velocity, rate of flow and viscous drag in arteries when the pressure gradient is known. J Physiol.
Merrill et al. (2005). Electrical stimulation of excitable tissue: design of efficacious and safe protocols. Journal of Neuroscience Methods.
Nicolelis et al. (2009). Principles of neural ensemble physiology underlying the operation of brain-machine interfaces. Nature Reviews Neuroscience.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
text:
Synthetic Biology and Genetic Engineering

Winfree (1967). Biological rhythms and the behavior of populations of coupled oscillators. Journal of Theoretical Biology.
Elowitz et al. (2000). A synthetic oscillatory network of transcriptional regulators. Nature.
Kitano (2002). Systems biology: a brief overview. Science.
Jinek et al. (2012). A Programmable Dual-RNA-Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity. Science.

title: Foundational and Milestone Papers
text:
Medical/Healthcare AI

Shortliffe and Buchanan (1975) A model of inexact reasoning in medicine. Mathematical Biosciences.
Krizhevsky et al. (2012). ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems.
Henry et al. (2015). A targeted real-time early warning score (TREWScore) for septic shock. Science Translational Medicine.
Gulshan et al. (2016). Development and Validation of a Deep Learning Algorithm for Detection of Diabetic Retinopathy in Retinal Fundus Photographs. JAMA.
Esteva et al. (2017). Dermatologist-level classification of skin cancer with deep neural networks. Nature.
Rajkomar et al. (2018). Scalable and accurate deep learning with electronic health records. npj Digital Medicine.
Obermeyer et al. (2019). Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations. Science.
Hannun et al. (2019). Cardiologist-level arrhythmia detection and classification in ambulatory electrocardiograms using a deep neural network. Nature Medicine.
Topol (2019). High-performance medicine: the convergence of human and artificial intelligence. Nature Medicine.
Stokes et al. (2020). A Deep Learning Approach to Antibiotic Discovery. Cell.
Jumper et al. (2021). Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold. Nature.
Acosta et al. (2022). Multimodal biomedical AI. Nature Medicine.
Singhal et al. (2023). Large language models encode clinical knowledge. Nature.
Chen et al. (2024). Towards a general-purpose foundation model for computational pathology. Nature Medicine.
